chunk_summary: Guidelines for milk volume limits, beverage choices, and meal structure in complementary feeding for children aged 6–24 months, emphasizing iron intake, dental health, and culturally appropriate alternatives.
learning_objectives: Understand milk volume recommendations for children aged 1–2 years to prevent iron deficiency.
Identify appropriate beverage choices for children under 24 months, including avoiding sugar-sweetened drinks.
Learn strategies to replace unhealthy snacks with nutrient-rich, culturally acceptable options.
Recognize the importance of meal structure and gradual night weaning for better feeding habits.
key_facts: Limit milk intake to about 2 cups (16 oz or 450–500 mL) per day for 1–2 year olds; excess intake (>24 oz/day) can cause iron deficiency.
Encourage milk consumption from an open cup rather than a bottle to reduce dental caries risk.
WHO 2023 guidelines allow full-fat animal milk for infants 6–11 months if formula or breast milk is unavailable, with strict handling/storage rules.
Avoid tea, coffee, caffeinated soft drinks, and sugar-sweetened beverages under 24 months; keep 100% fruit juice occasional and small.
Replace unhealthy snacks like biscuits and sweetened drinks with nutrient-rich options such as fruit slices, curd, thick porridge, soft idli, egg, or dal-based snacks.
reference_quote: After 12 months, pasteurized whole cow’s milk can be part of a diverse diet, but it should not replace complementary foods. Limit milk to about 2 cups (16 oz or 450–500 mL) per day for 1–2 year olds; intakes above about 24 oz/day are associated with iron deficiency when solids are displaced. — CDC Infant and Toddler Nutrition
document_key_topics: Milk volume limits for children 6–24 months
Beverage counselling and avoidance of sweetened drinks
